source_papers: Parpola, A. (1994a) Deciphering the Indus Script. Cambridge UP.
Parpola, A. (2010) A Dravidian solution to the Indus script problem. Coimbatore.
Parpola, A. (1994b) sign list = Parpola 1994a fig. 5.1, pp. 70-78.
Mahadevan, I. (1977) The Indus Script: Texts, Concordance and Tables.
Wells, B. (2015) Epigraphic Approaches to Indus Writing.
method_note: These are Parpola's hypothesized Dravidian rebus phonetic values for Indus signs, NOT consensus readings. The most-cited and most-defended is the 'fish'-family of signs reading as Dravidian *miin* (fish/star, with homonymy). Confidence tiers: 'high' = Parpola defends across 1994 + 2010 + 2018; 'medium' = Parpola proposes once or in passing; 'low' = speculative; 'none' = no Parpola proposal in our extracted sources.

47.source: Parpola 2010 (the central case study); Parpola 1994a Ch.10

60.source: Laursen 2010 fn 2 cites this as 'badly drawn fish'; Parpola family

145.source: Parpola 1994a fig. 5.1; co-occurs with numerical signs to form astral names

147.source: Parpola 1994b sign list; Laursen 2010 fn 2 (Janabiyah seal pos. 1b)

311.source: Parpola 2010 ('fig + fish' compound); 'V' sign per Parpola fig. 5.1 no. 311

16.gloss: rare in Indus Valley, common in Near East
16.source: Laursen 2010 fn 2 attests as Janabiyah seal pos. 5; sequence 16-145 at start of Kalibangan K-69 to K-75
16.noted_pattern: 16-145 sequence repeats in Kalibangan K-69 to K-75 (Joshi & Parpola 1987)

364.source: Laursen 2010 fn 2 attests as Janabiyah seal pos. 2; co-occurs with 16 on Mohenjo-Daro M-798 unicorn seal (Shah & Parpola 1991:68)

126.source: Laursen 2010 fn 2 attests as Janabiyah seal pos. 4; alternates 125 and 128
126.uncertainty_note: Parpola flagged 'very uncertain' even in his attested reading

53.source: Laursen 2010 fn 2 attests as Janabiyah seal pos. 1a (alt: 60 'fish')

1.source: Parpola 1994a ch.13; cf. Indus 'man' sign with raised arm

99.source: Parpola 1994a; sign depicting bowman or bow

janabiyah_seal_reading.summary: 3 confirmed miin (fish/star) + 1 likely miin alternate (53|60) + 3 uncertain/unread signs. The reading is dominated by the 'miin' element, suggesting a name with multiple star-or-fish references. Per Parpola's Dravidian hypothesis, names like aru-miin (Pleiades), eZu-miin (Ursa Major), vaTa-miin (north star) all use the miin morpheme. Three miin in one personal name is consistent with a high-status name carrying multiple astral epithets.
janabiyah_seal_reading.expected_attested_form: Sumerian/Akkadian transliteration would render *miin* as syllables like 'mi-in', 'me-en', 'mi-na', 'me-na'. Search Mesopotamian PNs for: mi-in, me-en, mi-na, mi-il, me-il (Sumerian rendering of Dravidian *miin*) or mu-li (Sumerian 'star').

search_strategy_for_phonetic_match: Generate all transliteration candidates for *miin*: mi-in, me-en, mi-na, me-na, mi-il, me-il
Search CDLI Meluhha-mention tablets for any PN containing one or more of these syllabic forms
Score candidates by: (a) number of *miin* phonemes matched, (b) attestation count, (c) period overlap with Janabiyah find-period (Early Dilmun ~2100-2000 BC)
